items.instruction: Is this directive stated correctly?
items.question_text: Avoid clearances which require super or heavy aircraft to use greater than normal taxiing power.
items.explanation: That directive appears in 3-7-3a.

items.instruction: Does this conditional rule match the paragraph?
items.question_text: When taxiing smaller aircraft or helicopters in the vicinity of larger aircraft or helicopters, use caution.
items.explanation: The note to 3-7-3 directs controllers to use caution when smaller aircraft or helicopters are taxiing near larger aircraft or helicopters.

items.instruction: Does this controller requirement match the paragraph?
items.question_text: Controllers may use the words rotor wash, jet blast, or prop wash when issuing cautionary advisories.
items.explanation: That option appears in the note to 3-7-3.

items.instruction: Which situation is specifically listed as one to avoid in a clearance?
items.question_text: Under 3-7-3, which condition is specifically listed as one that should not be created by a clearance?

choices.text: Small aircraft or helicopters taxiing in close proximity to taxiing or hover-taxi helicopters.
Small aircraft holding short on a parallel taxiway while a helicopter departs another runway.
Super aircraft taxiing at normal power on an assigned route.
Helicopters taxiing behind fixed-wing aircraft after receiving wake turbulence cautionary advisories.

items.explanation: Per 3-7-3b, avoid clearances that require small aircraft or helicopters to taxi in close proximity to taxiing or hover-taxi helicopters.

items.instruction: What should you do?
items.situation: A small helicopter needs to taxi near a CH-53 that is hover-taxiing. Another controller suggests keeping the route tight and skipping any cautionary advisory because both aircraft are already in sight of each other.
items.para_context: Under 3-7-3, avoid clearances that require small aircraft or helicopters to taxi in close proximity to taxiing or hover-taxi helicopters. The note adds that controllers may use rotor wash, jet blast, or prop wash in cautionary advisories.

choices.text: Avoid the close-proximity clearance and, when appropriate, issue a cautionary advisory such as rotor wash.
Issue the close-proximity taxi route because visual contact between pilots removes the restriction.
Use the route as planned, but replace the cautionary advisory with an immediate takeoff clearance.
Allow the routing as long as the small helicopter taxis faster than normal to clear the area.

items.explanation: Per 3-7-3, avoid clearances that create the close-proximity taxi situation and use cautionary advisories when appropriate.

items.instruction: Is this directive stated correctly?
items.question_text: Advise your supervisor or flow controller as soon as possible when you delay or expect to delay aircraft.
items.explanation: That directive appears in 4-6-3a.

items.instruction: Does this conditional rule match the paragraph?
items.question_text: When arrival delays reach or are anticipated to reach 30 minutes, the responsible center or approach control facility must issue total delay information as soon as possible after the aircraft enters its area.
items.explanation: The EN ROUTE and TERMINAL requirements in 4-6-3b apply when arrival delays reach or are anticipated to reach 30 minutes.

items.instruction: Does this controller requirement match the paragraph?
items.question_text: Unless a pilot requests delay information, the actions in subparagraphs 1 and 2 may be omitted when total delay information is available via ATIS.
items.explanation: That exception appears in 4-6-3b3.

items.instruction: What should you do?
items.situation: Arrival delays at the destination are expected to reach 35 minutes. An inbound aircraft has just entered the center's area for transfer to approach control, and no ATIS exception applies. Another controller wants to wait until the aircraft is near the boundary of the approach control area before issuing any delay information.
items.para_context: Under 4-6-3, when arrival delays reach or are anticipated to reach 30 minutes, the responsible center must issue total delay information as soon as possible after the aircraft enters the center's area, and whenever possible the first center controller to communicate with the aircraft should issue it.

choices.text: Issue the total delay information as soon as possible now, preferably as the first center controller communicating with the aircraft.
Wait until the aircraft is about to enter the approach control area so the delay information is more current.
Hold the information until the pilot requests it, because center delay advisories are always pilot-initiated.
Delay the advisory until the airport ATIS has been updated, because center controllers do not issue total delay information directly.

items.explanation: Per 4-6-3b1, the responsible center issues total delay information as soon as possible after the aircraft enters the center's area.

items.instruction: Does this spacing statement match the paragraph?
items.question_text: Keep speed adjustments to the minimum necessary to achieve or maintain required or desired spacing.
items.explanation: That requirement appears in the opening sentence of 5-7-1.

items.instruction: Is this directive stated correctly?
items.question_text: Terminate speed adjustments when no longer needed.
items.explanation: That directive appears in the opening sentence of 5-7-1.

items.instruction: Which aircraft is specifically listed as one to which speed adjustment should not be assigned?
items.question_text: Under 5-7-1, which aircraft is specifically listed as one that should not be assigned speed adjustment without pilot consent or another exception?

choices.text: An aircraft at or above FL 390 without pilot consent.
An aircraft below FL 390 that is complying with an assigned STAR.
An aircraft on downwind that has not yet reached the final approach fix.
An aircraft being vectored after a published speed restriction is canceled.

items.explanation: Per 5-7-1b1, do not assign speed adjustment to aircraft at or above FL 390 without pilot consent.

items.instruction: Does this controller requirement match the paragraph?
items.question_text: At the time approach clearance or a climb via or descend via clearance is issued, previously assigned speeds must be restated if required.
items.explanation: That requirement appears in 5-7-1c.

items.instruction: Does this conditional rule match the paragraph?
items.question_text: When an aircraft is vectored off a SID or STAR, you must treat the published speed restriction on that procedure as canceled.
items.explanation: Per 5-7-1e, a published speed restriction on a SID or STAR is canceled when the aircraft is vectored off or approved to deviate from the procedure.

items.instruction: What should you do?
items.situation: An aircraft is being vectored off a STAR that includes a published speed restriction. You still need 210 knots to maintain spacing. Another controller says the published STAR speed remains in effect, so no further speed assignment is necessary.
items.para_context: Under 5-7-1, a speed restriction published as part of a SID or STAR is canceled when an aircraft is vectored off the procedure or a deviation is approved. If necessary, assign a speed with the vector or approval to deviate.

choices.text: Assign the needed speed with the vector, because the published STAR speed restriction is canceled once the aircraft is vectored off the procedure.
Rely on the published STAR speed restriction because it remains in effect until the aircraft is cleared for the approach.
Cancel all speed control immediately because vectors prohibit any further assigned speed.
Wait until the aircraft reaches the final approach fix before assigning any new speed.

items.explanation: Per 5-7-1e, vectoring an aircraft off the SID or STAR cancels the published speed restriction, so assign a speed if needed.

items.instruction: Is this directive stated correctly?
items.question_text: On initial contact with the final controller, ask the aircraft for a communication check.
items.explanation: That is the body requirement in 5-10-9.

items.instruction: Does this citation match the paragraph's reference?
items.question_text: This paragraph cites FAA Order JO 7110.65, Para 5-9-2, Final Approach Course Interception.
items.explanation: The paragraph cites Paragraph 5-9-2 among its references.

items.instruction: What should you do?
items.situation: An aircraft checks in with the final controller. Another controller says there is no need to verify the audio quality because the aircraft already contacted approach on the previous frequency.
items.para_context: Under 5-10-9, on initial contact with the final controller, ask the aircraft for a communication check using the published final-controller phraseology.

choices.text: Ask for the communication check, such as by transmitting the final-controller call and 'How do you hear me?'
Assume the communications are satisfactory because the aircraft was already talking to another controller.
Wait until the aircraft is established on final before checking communications.
Skip the communication check unless the pilot reports a radio problem first.

items.explanation: Per 5-10-9, the final controller asks the aircraft for a communication check on initial contact.

items.instruction: Which function is specifically listed for terminal automation?
items.question_text: Under 5-14-3, which function is specifically listed as a use of terminal automation?
items.explanation: Ground speed is one of the listed functions in 5-14-3.

items.instruction: Does this scope or responsibility statement match the paragraph?
items.question_text: In addition to other uses specified herein, terminal automation may be used for tracking, tagging, handoff, altitude information, coordination, ground speed, and identification.
items.explanation: That sentence restates the listed functions in 5-14-3.

items.instruction: Does this citation match the paragraph's reference?
items.question_text: This paragraph cites FAA Order JO 7110.65, Para 5-2-21, Altitude Filters.
items.explanation: The paragraph cites Paragraph 5-2-21.

items.instruction: Does this controller requirement match the paragraph?
items.question_text: Two-letter ICAO designators or three-letter designators, as appropriate, must be used unless program limitations dictate the use of a single letter alpha prefix.
items.explanation: That requirement appears in 5-14-5a.

items.instruction: Does this scope or responsibility statement match the paragraph?
items.question_text: Information displayed must be in accordance with national orders and specified in local directives.
items.explanation: That requirement appears in 5-14-5c.

items.instruction: Does this system or capability statement match the paragraph?
items.question_text: The tower display workstation supports ADS-B indicator display only when it is operating in Fused Display Mode.
items.explanation: That note appears under 5-14-5d.

items.instruction: Does this citation match the paragraph's reference?
items.question_text: This paragraph cites FAA Order JO 7110.65, Para 3-1-9, Use of Tower Radar Displays.
items.explanation: The paragraph cites Paragraph 3-1-9.

items.instruction: What should you do?
items.situation: The ASR supporting the airport's ASDE system is out of service, and MLAT is unavailable. A tower controller responsible for aircraft on approach says the ADS-B indicator can remain off because the workstation is still showing fused targets from normal operations.
items.para_context: Under 5-14-5d, during outages of the ASR that supports an ASDE system when MLAT is inoperative or not present, the tower position responsible for aircraft on approach to the airport must enable the ADS-B indicator on the tower display workstation.

choices.text: Enable the ADS-B indicator on the tower display workstation for the position responsible for aircraft on approach.
Leave the ADS-B indicator off unless a pilot reports lost target information.
Wait until the ASR outage exceeds 30 minutes before enabling the ADS-B indicator.
Use the ADS-B indicator only for departures because the paragraph does not apply to approach traffic.

items.explanation: Per 5-14-5d, the tower position responsible for aircraft on approach must enable the ADS-B indicator during the listed outage condition.

items.instruction: Does this conditional rule match the paragraph?
items.question_text: When a CA or MCI alert is displayed, evaluate the reason for the alert without delay and take appropriate action.
items.explanation: That requirement appears in 5-14-6a.

items.instruction: Does this controller requirement match the paragraph?
items.question_text: If another controller is involved in the alert, initiate coordination to ensure an effective course of action, unless immediate action is dictated.
items.explanation: That requirement appears in 5-14-6b.

items.instruction: Which operation is specifically listed as an example where approved separation criteria do not apply?
items.question_text: Under the note in 5-14-6, which operation is specifically listed as one where approved separation criteria do not apply?

choices.text: Standard Class C sequencing to the primary airport.
Normal radar handoff procedures between TRACON sectors.
Routine IFR separation inside published holding patterns.

items.explanation: The note to 5-14-6 identifies ADC practice intercept operations and air shows as examples.

items.instruction: Does this citation match the paragraph's reference?
items.question_text: This paragraph cites FAA Order JO 7110.65, Para 2-1-6, Safety Alert.
items.explanation: The paragraph cites Paragraph 2-1-6.

items.instruction: What should you do?
items.situation: A CA alert appears and another controller is handling one of the aircraft involved. There is time to coordinate before any immediate avoidance instruction is necessary. Another controller says to handle the alert at your position only and suppress it for the other position later without coordination.
items.para_context: Under 5-14-6, evaluate a displayed CA or MCI alert without delay, coordinate with another involved controller unless immediate action is dictated, and do not suppress or inhibit a CA or MCI alert at or for another control position without coordination.

choices.text: Coordinate with the other controller and do not suppress or inhibit the alert for another position without that coordination.
Suppress the alert for the other position first, then coordinate after traffic is resolved.
Ignore the alert unless the other controller specifically requests coordination.
Delay all action because coordination is always required before you can evaluate the reason for the alert.

items.explanation: Per 5-14-6, evaluate the alert without delay and coordinate with the other controller unless immediate action is dictated; suppressing or inhibiting at another position also requires coordination.

items.instruction: Is this directive stated correctly?
items.question_text: Apply Class C service procedures within the designated Class C airspace and the associated outer area.
items.explanation: That directive appears in the opening sentence of 7-8-1.

items.instruction: Does this scope or responsibility statement match the paragraph?
items.question_text: Class C services are used to keep ATC informed of all aircraft within Class C airspace, not to exclude operations.
items.explanation: That scope statement appears in 7-8-1.

items.instruction: Does this controller requirement match the paragraph?
items.question_text: Two-way radio communications and an operational transponder are normally required for operations within Class C airspace, but operations without them may be conducted by LOA, facility directive, or special arrangement with the controlling facility.
items.explanation: That requirement and exception appear in 7-8-1.

items.instruction: What should you do?
items.situation: A VFR aircraft with an inoperative transponder needs to enter the Class C area under a standing special arrangement with the Class C controlling facility. Another controller says the operation must be denied because a radio and transponder are always mandatory in Class C.
items.para_context: Under 7-8-1, apply Class C service procedures within the designated Class C airspace and the associated outer area. Two-way radio communications and an operational transponder are normally required within Class C airspace, but operations without radio communications or transponder can be conducted by LOA, facility directive, or special arrangement with the controlling facility.

choices.text: Allow the operation under the approved special arrangement and apply Class C procedures as appropriate, even though radio or transponder equipment is normally required.
Deny the operation automatically because Class C equipment requirements have no exceptions under any local arrangement.
Treat the aircraft as excluded from all ATC services because Class C procedures apply only to aircraft with fully functioning equipment.
Allow the aircraft only in the outer area because special arrangements can never apply inside designated Class C airspace.

items.explanation: Per 7-8-1, radio and transponder equipment are normally required, but operations without them may be conducted by LOA, facility directive, or special arrangement with the Class C controlling facility.

items.instruction: Is this directive stated correctly?
items.question_text: Provide Class C services to all participating aircraft in the outer area.
items.explanation: That directive appears in 7-8-2c.

items.instruction: Does this conditional rule match the paragraph?
items.question_text: When an outage affects the preferred radar sensor, advise aircraft that Class C services are not available and, if alternate radar coverage is sufficient, continue to provide radar services to that extent.
items.explanation: That requirement appears in 7-8-2e.

items.instruction: Which service is specifically listed as part of Class C services?
items.question_text: Under 7-8-2, which service is specifically listed as part of Class C services?

choices.text: Mandatory traffic advisories and safety alerts between VFR aircraft.
Automatic vectors to final for every VFR aircraft in the outer area.
Positive separation between all VFR aircraft and terrain.
Holding instructions for every aircraft before entering the Class C area.

items.explanation: Per 7-8-2a4, Class C services include mandatory traffic advisories and safety alerts between VFR aircraft.

items.instruction: Does this citation match the paragraph's reference?
items.question_text: This paragraph cites FAA Order JO 7110.65, Para 7-2-1, Visual Separation.
items.explanation: The paragraph cites Paragraph 7-2-1.

items.instruction: What should you do?
items.situation: The preferred radar sensor for a Class C area fails. Backup radar covers most of the airspace, but one portion has limited coverage. A controller proposes suspending all Class C radar service throughout the area.
items.para_context: Under 7-8-2, provide Class C services to all aircraft in Class C airspace and all participating aircraft in the outer area. If an outage affects the preferred radar sensor, advise aircraft that Class C services are not available and, if appropriate, when to contact the tower. If other radar resources are available and coverage is sufficient, continue radar services to that extent. Limited radar coverage in one portion of the Class C area does not justify denial of Class C radar service in the entire area.

choices.text: Continue radar services where alternate radar coverage is sufficient, advise aircraft appropriately where Class C service is unavailable, and do not deny the entire area solely because one portion has limited coverage.
Suspend all Class C radar service immediately because any preferred-sensor outage requires shutting down the entire Class C operation.
Continue providing full Class C services everywhere without advising aircraft about the outage because backup coverage exists somewhere in the area.
Hold all arriving and departing aircraft until the preferred radar sensor returns, because Class C aircraft should always be held during any outage.

items.explanation: Per 7-8-2 and its note, service continues to the extent coverage is sufficient, and limited coverage in one portion does not justify denying radar service throughout the entire Class C area.

items.instruction: Is this directive stated correctly?
items.question_text: Inform the pilot to remain outside Class C airspace until conditions permit the services to be provided.
items.explanation: That directive appears in the final sentence of 7-8-4.

items.instruction: Does this citation match the paragraph's reference?
items.question_text: This paragraph cites FAA Order JO 7110.65, Para 7-2-1, Visual Separation.
items.explanation: The paragraph cites Paragraph 7-2-1.

items.instruction: Which pilot readback is correct?
items.clearance: N123AB REMAIN OUTSIDE CHARLIE AIRSPACE AND STANDBY.

choices.text: N123AB remaining outside Charlie airspace and standing by.
N123AB entering Charlie airspace and standing by.
N123AB cleared into Charlie airspace, standby.
N123AB remain outside Bravo airspace and standby.

items.explanation: Per 7-8-4 phraseology, when Class C services cannot be provided immediately, instruct the pilot to remain outside Charlie airspace and standby.

items.instruction: One word in this phraseology is incorrect. Tap it.
items.original_phrase: REMAIN OUTSIDE CHARLIE AIRSPACE AND STANDBY.
items.display_text: REMAIN INSIDE CHARLIE AIRSPACE AND STANDBY
items.explanation: Per 7-8-4 phraseology, the aircraft must remain outside Charlie airspace until services can be provided.

items.instruction: What should you do?
items.situation: A VFR aircraft calls approach just before entering Class C airspace. Traffic and workload prevent you from providing Class C services immediately.
items.para_context: Under 7-8-4, pilots must establish two-way radio communications before entering Class C airspace. If the controller responds with the aircraft call sign and 'standby,' communications are established. If workload or traffic conditions prevent immediate Class C services, inform the pilot to remain outside Class C airspace until conditions permit the services to be provided.

choices.text: Respond with the aircraft call sign and instruct it to remain outside Charlie airspace and standby until services can be provided.
Tell the pilot that any radio contact automatically authorizes entry into Class C even if services cannot yet be provided.
Do not answer until ready to provide full Class C service because communications are not considered established by a standby response.
Authorize entry into Class C and then sort out services after the aircraft is inside the airspace.

items.explanation: Per 7-8-4, if services cannot be provided immediately, the pilot must be told to remain outside Class C airspace until conditions permit them.

items.question_text: Under 3-7-3, which cautionary advisory term may controllers use when appropriate?
items.explanation: The note to 3-7-3 says controllers may use rotor wash, jet blast, or prop wash when issuing cautionary advisories.

choices.text: Rotor wash, jet blast, or prop wash.
Crosswind, wake zone, or visual caution.
Taxi delay, departure hold, or runway caution.
Wingtip watch, low rotor, or braking caution.

items.question_text: Under 3-7-3, avoid clearances that require super or heavy aircraft to use greater than normal taxiing power.
items.explanation: That requirement appears in 3-7-3a.

items.question_text: Under 4-6-3, when arrival delays reach or are anticipated to reach 30 minutes, when must the responsible center issue total delay information?
items.explanation: Per 4-6-3b1, the responsible center issues total delay information as soon as possible after the aircraft enters the center's area.

choices.text: As soon as possible after the aircraft enters the center's area.
Before the aircraft enters the center's area.
Only after the aircraft enters final approach.
Only if the pilot specifically requests it after landing.

items.question_text: Under 4-6-3, unless a pilot requests delay information, the actions in subparagraphs 1 and 2 may be omitted when total delay information is available via ATIS.
items.explanation: That exception appears in 4-6-3b3.

items.question_text: Under 5-7-1, to which aircraft must speed adjustment not be assigned without pilot consent?
items.explanation: Per 5-7-1b1, do not assign speed adjustment to aircraft at or above FL 390 without pilot consent.

choices.text: An aircraft at or above FL 390.
An aircraft descending on a STAR below FL 240.
An aircraft being vectored after canceling a STAR speed restriction.
An aircraft on downwind outside the final approach fix.

items.question_text: Under 5-7-1, approach clearances or climb via or descend via clearances cancel previously assigned speeds unless the speeds are restated.
items.explanation: That requirement appears in 5-7-1c and 5-7-1d.

items.question_text: Under 5-10-9, what should the final controller ask on initial contact?
items.explanation: The phraseology in 5-10-9 ends with 'How do you hear me?' as the communication check.

items.question_text: Under 5-10-9, on initial contact with the final controller, ask the aircraft for a communication check.
items.explanation: That is the body requirement in 5-10-9.

items.question_text: Under 5-14-3, which function is specifically listed as a use of terminal automation?
items.explanation: Per 5-14-3, listed functions include tracking, tagging, handoff, altitude information, coordination, ground speed, and identification.

items.question_text: Under 5-14-3, terminal automation may be used for altitude information.
items.explanation: Altitude information is one of the listed functions in 5-14-3.

items.question_text: Under 5-14-5, what must the tower position responsible for aircraft on approach do during an outage of the ASR that supports an ASDE system when MLAT is inoperative or not present?
items.explanation: Per 5-14-5d, the ADS-B indicator must be enabled on the tower display workstation.

choices.text: Enable the ADS-B indicator on the tower display workstation.
Disable all target tags until the ASR returns.
Switch every aircraft to voice-only tracking and stop using the workstation.
Transfer all approach aircraft to the center immediately.

items.question_text: Under the note in 5-14-5, when will the ADS-B indicator display on the tower display workstation?
items.explanation: The note says the ADS-B indicator displays only if the TDW is operating in Fused Display Mode.

choices.text: Only when the TDW is operating in Fused Display Mode.
Only when MLAT and ASR are both fully operational.
Only when the workstation is configured for departures only.
Only after local directives are suspended.

items.question_text: Under 5-14-5, information displayed must be in accordance with national orders and specified in local directives.
items.explanation: That requirement appears in 5-14-5c.

items.question_text: Under 5-14-6, what is required before a CA or MCI alert may be suppressed or inhibited at or for another control position?
items.explanation: Per 5-14-6c4, suppression or inhibition at or for another control position requires coordination.

choices.text: Coordination with the other control position.
A pilot request to reduce alerts.
Completion of a safety alert log entry only.
Approval from the next controller after the alert is suppressed.

items.question_text: Under 5-14-6, the inhibit function must only be used to inhibit the display of CA for aircraft routinely engaged in operations where approved separation criteria do not apply.
items.explanation: That limitation appears in 5-14-6c2.

choices.text: False because the inhibit function may be used for any routine IFR operation.
False because only the suppress function may ever be used on CA alerts.
False because inhibit applies only to MCI alerts, not CA alerts.

items.question_text: Under 5-14-6, when a CA or MCI alert is displayed, evaluate the reason for the alert without delay and take appropriate action.
items.explanation: That requirement appears in 5-14-6a.
